channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: Can Spainâs teamwork overcome Lionel Messiâs genius?
item.description: The World Cup finalists are strikingly different teamsÂ

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Make America worse at sports
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our US editor, on why the fantasy of athletic greatness has becomeÂ a plague on American life

item.title: The Climate Issue newsletter: The dangers of global dimming
item.description: Oliver Morton, our planetary affairs editor,Â reflects on a warming Earth

item.title: The degradation of the Department of Justice
item.description: Our weekly podcast on America. This week, Donald Trump reinvents an institution

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Itâs too darn hot
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: The final score on Sir Keir Starmerâs two years in office
item.description: Some progress, but all too plodding

item.title: Should you microdose GLP-1 drugs for weight loss?
item.description: Possibly. But only under medical supervision

item.title: Boomers have the good life, but it could be longer
item.description: A single chart shows why they have missed out on extra years

item.title: Pete Hegsethâs testosterone-screening plan is unusual, not crazy
item.description: Soldiers seem to be at particular risk of hypogonadism

item.title: Tours of British post-war housing are a quiet hit
item.description: Perambulations of the anti-Instagram kind

item.title: What to watch this week
item.description: Our list this week features tragic heroes and tragic adaptations

item.title: Youâve come a long way, Bibi: Israelâs crucial election
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: takeaways from the World Cup and the worldâs largest iceberg melts away at last

item.title: The doctors keeping Cuba on life support
item.description: Medics are the countryâs most valuable export. Now Trump has his sights on them

item.title: Russia is losing its grip on Crimea
item.description: Ukraineâs drone offensive is making it harder to supply and defend the vital peninsula

item.title: The man reinventing a trillion-dollar drugmaker
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, an interview with David Ricks, CEO of Eli Lilly

item.title: Cartoon: Continuing uncertainty over the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Humphrey Smith dictated what a good pub should be
item.description: The lord high brewer of Tadcaster died on June 19th, aged 81

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
Emo culture was not just a phase, as it turns out
item.description: The business of misery is thriving as emo bands go back on tour

item.title: George Sandâs life was a heady mix of sex and serious fiction
item.description: A new biography seeks to restore the French novelistâs place in the literary canon

item.title: Why Sir Christopher Nolan inspires such devotionâand contempt
item.description: His films are ambitious and technically brilliant, but often emotionally detached

item.title: A mystery street artist is taking over cities
item.description: You can hunt for his mosaics in Paris and beyond

item.title: How SK Hynix became the king of advanced memory chips
item.description: Its advantages will not protect it if demand falters

item.title: How to sell a kettle
item.description: Brand positioning reaches boiling point

item.title: China wants to end AI romances
item.description: They are having too much impact on young peopleâs lives

item.title: A real but selective crackdown on Cambodiaâs scam industry
item.description: Sanctions from America and pressure from China have forced a reckoning

item.title: The Panama Canal is growing more important
item.description: And the challenges to it are getting more acute

item.title: The biggest schools training Americaâs doctors are in the Caribbean
item.description: The industry has a seedy underbelly

item.title: A hit new show takes on polygamy in modern South Africa
item.description: âThe Polygamistâ is trashy yet perceptive about family, patriarchy and inequality

item.title: Americaâs other battle with Iran
item.description: The two countries are vying for influence in Iraq

item.title: Lindsey Graham tried to save the wrong countries from Donald Trump
item.description: He should have put American democracy first

item.title: In praise of Scandinaviaâs risky and dirty playgrounds
item.description: Kids stand to gain from a few bumps, bruises and mud-splatters

item.title: Democrats challenge a big Hollywood tie-up
item.description: A dozen states are suing to block Paramountâs merger with Warner Bros

item.title: Sweden squashed migration. The populist right wants to go further
item.description: The Sweden Democrats could be heading for a bigger role in government

item.title: Opening the border between Spain and Gibraltar
item.description: The deal offers hope for a more pragmatic relationship between Britain and Europe

item.title: Why Berlinâs airport is still completely hopeless
item.description: Blame passengers, lobbying, history and geography

item.title: Sir Keirâs bucket listÂ
item.description: The prime ministerâs final few weeks sum him upÂ

item.title: Europe seems set to ease its carbon pricing
item.description: Green goals are running into fears about competitiveness

item.title: Lessons from a cat about to outlast yet another prime minister
item.description: Number 10âs canny feline can teach Andy Burnham a thing or two

item.title: Glasgow, rescuer of the Commonwealth Games, needs saving too
item.description: The Scottish Nationalists are keen to take power from London but not to hand any to Glasgow

item.title: Can Andy Burnham keep his own MPs under control?
item.description: Britainâs prime-minister-to-be faces a big task to maintain his authority

item.title: Would Europeans fight, if it came to that?
item.description: Putting boots on the ground would not help Ukraine, but it might help Europe find the stomach for war, writes Nathalie Tocci

item.title: Israelâs future hangs in the balance
item.description: The countryâs coming election may be its most important ever

item.title: The United States is once again in a state of rebellion
item.description: Also this week, the backlash against AI, homelessness, Eisenhower and roads, oil predictions

item.title: America should stop making it so hard to have fun
item.description: Bureaucracy is nightlifeâs toughest bouncer

item.title: Chinaâs rulers have a woman problem
item.description: Antagonism between the sexes does nothing to address Chinaâs demographic problems

item.title: Business
Politics
The man who would change Russia?
item.description: On the contrary, Andrey Melnichenkoâs essay is about how to make sure nothing changes at all, says Boris Bondarev

item.title: What if revolution in Whitehall is overrated?
item.description: The low-hanging-fruit school of reforming the British state

item.title: In it to bin it: Nigel Farage v Count Binface
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: fed-up Africans turn to Starlink for their internet and a very silly âThe Odysseyâ adaptation

item.title: An unreliable America is drawing Asiaâs middle powers closer
item.description: A burst of Indian diplomacy exemplifies the trend

item.title: Sovereign AI, independent of America and China, is a pipe dream
item.description: But a degree of protection from coercion remains possible

item.title: Ukraineâs reformist defence minister is ousted
item.description: The generals pushed back at his efforts to modernise the army

item.title: Donald Trumpâs transformation of the Department of Justice will be hard to undo
item.description: Political prosecutions are just part of a more radical reinvention

item.title: How to shrink the Fedâs $7trn balance-sheet
item.description: Kevin Warsh will struggle to reverse the effects of bond-buying

item.title: Donald Trumpâs gutting of the Department of Justice
item.description: What the degraded institution means for America

item.title: Itâs too darn hot. Blame global dimming
item.description: Earth is absorbing a lot more sunshine

item.title: Can Kevin Warshâs Fed force 5 reimagine monetary policymaking?
item.description: The new chairman enlists heavy-hitters to fight a handful of gnarly problems

item.title: What faces reveal about humanity
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: Donald Trumpâs blind alley
item.description: Americaâs president looks bereft of good options for solving the stand-off in the Gulf

item.title: The rate at which Earth is absorbing energy is alarming scientists
item.description: Reflections on a warming planet

item.title: Earth is dimming
item.description: The planet is reflecting less light back into space. That is making the world hotter

item.title: How to make AI safeâand lessen dependence on America and China
item.description: State-backed efforts to catch up with frontier models are doomed

item.title: Eli Lilly is reinventing the pharma business
item.description: The worldâs largest drugmaker is betting big on preventive medicinesâand learning from big tech

item.title: Chinaâs trade gap is narrowing. And other surprises
item.description: The worldâs second-biggest economy is stumbling into fiscal austerity

item.title: Meet the committee to buy Europe
item.description: The continentâs dealmakers deserve much more attention

item.title: Violence is reshaping British political life
item.description: The shocking murder of Ann Widdecombe will only reinforce the trend

item.title: How Europe can compete on AI
item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. The boss of Mistral AI argues that models donât have to be super-intelligentânor Americanâto be useful

item.title: How to preserve Africaâs architectural gems
item.description: Development and restoration must go hand in hand

item.title: A very silly adaptation of âThe Odysseyâ
item.description: Sir Christopher Nolanâs film is emblematic of its time

item.title: Xi Jinping expels another leader from his top team
item.description: He has set a record for booting out members of the Politburo

item.title: Argentina v England is one of the most intense rivalries in sport
item.description: The two countries have not played each other in over two decades

item.description: Also on the daily podcast: why Indiaâs government websites are so awful and the adults going back to summer camp

item.description: Parentsâ taste in baby names is changing, writes Hugo Gye, our British political correspondent

item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: When Chinaâs open-source AI is a trap
item.description: Americaâs quest for AI dominance is scary. China is not the solution

item.title: Cities are rethinking what happens after dark
item.description: Making it easier to party is becoming serious public policy

item.title: Americaâs Hormuz brinkmanship is worsening a global fuel crunch
item.description: Rising oil prices are only part of the problem

item.title: Britainâs bookies have weathered a tricky World Cup
item.description: But bigger problems are stacking up

item.description: To see a countryâs financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.description: Our weekly podcast on China. This week, what is it like to prepare for the gaokao?

item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, argues that the conflict is not âforgottenâ but âneglectedâ

item.description: The Google DeepMind co-founder sets out his vision in an exclusive interview

item.description: Oliver Carroll, our Ukraine correspondent, assesses the performance of the countryâs defence ministerÂ

item.description: Wives who outearn their husbands remain rare in America

item.description: But building a new community is harder than it looks

item.title: Ukraineâs reforming defence minister is under fire
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov has been trying to reform the army. The generals are pushing back

item.description: Its latest campaign wonât solve its debt woes, but inches in the right direction

item.title: Is Aung San Suu Kyi dead?
item.description: Myanmarâs jailed leader has not been seen since 2022

item.description: No other rich democracy hands as many decisions directly to its voters

item.description: South Carolinaâs senior senator is dead at 71

item.description: Prosperity and infrastructure development have not come with improved safety

item.description: It has spread to five provinces in Congo and may soon reach South Sudan

item.description: Its payments firms may be the first casualties

item.description: But online misogyny is only one corner of a much bigger problem

item.description: The merchants are becoming princes, writes Tim OâReilly

item.description: Alexandra Suich Bass, our culture editor, examines whether the uber-rich pursue Old Master paintings

item.description: A nice, hot bath can go a long way

item.description: England invented the sport. But its modern centre of gravity lies elsewhere

item.description: The precious work, insured for $1bn, is bound for Britain. Itâs more famous there than in France

item.description: Jin Mingri formerly ran a large house church in Beijing

item.description: Life is suffering; the Buddha can help

item.description: But its dams have flooded it with debt and other problems

item.description: The frontier complicates disagreements between indigenous groups

item.description: Stalling agricultural productivity makes it look unlikely
